title: SEGMENT 2 — Sydney Brenner and the deliberate choice of the worm

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — Sydney Brenner and the deliberate choice of the worm

In 1965, a molecular biologist named Sydney Brenner sat down in his office at the MRC Laboratory of Molecular Biology in Cambridge, England, and wrote what is now one of the most famous documents in twentieth-century biology: a memo, addressed to Max Perutz, the head of the lab, in which he announced that he wanted to switch his research program from the molecular biology of bacteria and bacteriophages — which by then was a mature field — to something much harder.
He wanted to understand how a complete nervous system is built.
How a body plan is laid out.
How the genes specify the cells, how the cells assemble into tissues, how the tissues into an animal.
He wanted, in short, to understand development and nervous systems at the level of individual cells and individual genes.
Brenner had a track record by then.
He had been part of the team that worked out the genetic code, that established the existence of messenger RNA, that nailed down many of the basic facts of molecular biology in the 1950s and 1960s.
He had earned the right to bet his career on a question.
And the question he chose — how a multicellular animal is built, gene by gene, cell by cell — was, at the time, considered essentially unanswerable.
Nobody knew where to start.
Brenner's bet — and this is the bet that, when it paid off, transformed the field — was that to answer that question, you had to pick the right animal.
Not the most famous animal.
Not the animal whose biology you cared about emotionally.
The right animal — meaning the one whose biology was simple enough that you could actually do the experiments, but rich enough that the answer would matter.
So Brenner went looking.
He surveyed possibilities.
Sea urchins were transparent and easy to fertilize but adults were too big.
Flatworms had a nervous system but were a mess to manipulate.
Various nematodes were considered.
Brenner eventually settled on a particular species of soil nematode called Caenorhabditis elegans — a tiny worm, about one millimeter long, that lives in temperate soils and eats bacteria.
Almost nobody outside of taxonomy had ever heard of it.
Brenner picked it on purpose.
Let me give you the list of features that made him pick it, because every one of these is a reason you can do experiments in worms that you can't do anywhere else.
One: it's tiny.
About one millimeter long as an adult, roughly the diameter of a pencil lead.
You can keep ten thousand worms on a single petri dish.
You can fit a year of experiments into a desk drawer.
The space constraints that limit mouse and zebrafish work essentially disappear in worms.
Two: it's transparent.
Under a microscope, you can see straight through the worm's body.
Every internal cell — every neuron, every muscle fiber, every gut cell — is visible without dissection.
You can watch the animal develop, watch its embryo divide, watch its cells migrate, all without cutting anything open.
The whole animal is its own histology slide.
Three: it has a tiny number of cells.
An adult hermaphrodite has exactly 959 somatic cells.
Exactly.
Every healthy adult worm has the same number, in the same positions, doing the same jobs.
That's an absurd statement for any other animal.
A mouse has tens of billions of cells, of which any two are arranged slightly differently.
A fly has tens of thousands.
A worm has 959, in the same arrangement every time.
We'll see in the next segment what you can do with that.
Four: it has a fast life cycle.
Three days from fertilized egg to reproductive adult, at room temperature.
A worm laid this morning is laying its own eggs by the weekend.
Compare with mouse at ten weeks, fish at three months, fly at ten days.
Worms are the speed runners of the animal kingdom for genetics.
Five: it has simple genetics.
Six chromosomes — five autosomes and one sex chromosome.
About a hundred million base pairs of genome — small for an animal.
About twenty thousand genes — comparable to humans, despite the simpler body.

Hermaphroditic — most adults are hermaphrodites that self-fertilize, which means a single founder animal can produce an entire homozygous line without you doing any crosses, and which makes maintaining mutant strains trivial.
The occasional male can be used for outcrossing when you want to combine genotypes.
Six: it freezes.
You can take a tube of worms, put them in glycerol, freeze them in liquid nitrogen, and thaw them out years later.
They come back to life and start reproducing.
So you can archive every mutant strain you ever isolate, indefinitely, in a freezer.
There is no comparable trick in mouse or fly.
The worm community maintains a stock center, the Caenorhabditis Genetics Center in Minneapolis, that holds tens of thousands of frozen strains, and any lab in the world can request a vial.

The analogy I want you to hold for Brenner's choice: Brenner's pick of the worm was the deliberate decision in 1965 — he picked the worm because it was simple enough to map every cell, complex enough to have interesting biology.
That's the sweet spot.
Too simple and you don't have the biology you care about — bacteria don't have development or nervous systems.
Too complex and you can't see what's going on — a mouse has too many cells, too many genes, too long a generation time to map at single-cell resolution.
The worm sits exactly in the middle.
Simple enough to map.
Complex enough to matter.
And Brenner didn't just pick the worm and start running experiments.

He spent years building the infrastructure — figuring out how to grow worms reproducibly on agar plates seeded with E. coli as food, working out the mutagenesis protocols, isolating the first mutant strains, building the genetic map.

He recruited a small group of colleagues and students who became, over the next decade, the founding generation of worm biology: John Sulston, Robert Horvitz, John White, and others whose names are going to come up in the segments ahead.
It's worth pausing on the strategic genius of this choice, because it's a model for how foundational science gets done.
Brenner did not start by asking, "What animal is most interesting?
" Many animals are interesting.
He started by asking, "What is the simplest experimental system that still contains the phenomenon I want to understand?
" The phenomenon — development, behavior, nervous system function — needed to be present.
But every additional layer of biological complexity, beyond the minimum needed to host that phenomenon, was a cost.
It made experiments harder, slower, more expensive.
So you find the simplest organism that still does the thing, and you commit to it.

Half a century later, C. elegans research has produced six Nobel Prizes, mapped a complete nervous system at the level of individual neurons and their connections, established the molecular basis of apoptosis (programmed cell death), revealed how aging is genetically controlled, and provided the system in which RNA interference was discovered.
Brenner's bet, in retrospect, looks like the best career decision in twentieth-century biology.
But at the time, in 1965, it looked like a senior molecular biologist deciding to study a worm nobody had heard of.
Strategic genius is hard to recognize until afterwards.
Now let's go look at the most extraordinary thing that came out of Brenner's worm — the complete cell lineage map.
